query: hormonal permissive action at onset of puberty

retrieved_docs.title: Precocious Puberty -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The HPG axis then becomes dormant until its subsequent activation in adolescence. The pulsatile release of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) from the hypothalamus stimulates the secretion of follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH) from the anterior pituitary gland. FSH and LH initiate spermatogenesis and the release of testosterone in males and oogenesis and the release of estradiol in females, respectively. Activation of the gonads is known as gonadarche.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Puberty -- Pathophysiology -- Precocious Puberty
retrieved_docs.content: Constitutional/idiopathic precocious puberty can occur in males and females but tends to be more prevalent in females. Precocious or premature puberty is considered idiopathic when a child has no familial links to premature development, and there is no other cause that explains the premature development of puberty. Constitutional precocious puberty can be linked to a familial tendency toward early development. Children with these disorders will respond to a GnRH stimulation test with pubertal levels of sex hormones, including FSH and LH. These children may have a bone age that is much higher than their chronological age. Additionally, all other causes of premature puberty must be ruled out, such as CNS pathology and elevated adrenal hormones. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Precocious Puberty -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Central precocious puberty

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Puberty -- Pathophysiology -- Precocious Puberty
retrieved_docs.content: Premature adrenarche correlates with the premature presence of pubic or axillary hair and possibly increased sebaceous gland activity, without other signs of puberty present, usually before six years of age. This is typically an isolated abnormality, and most children go on to develop the other signs of puberty at a normal age. Plasma DHEAS is usually elevated to pubertal levels, while FSH, LH, estradiol, and testosterone are typically at levels found in prepubertal children. Other studies, such as a GnRH stimulation test, will show prepubertal results. An ACTH stimulation test may help exclude congenital adrenal hyperplasia, which may present with similar symptoms. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Development -- At Puberty:
retrieved_docs.content: This temporary pause in GnRH release ends at puberty, and recent studies have shown that Kisspeptin neurons are responsible for activation of hypothalamic-gonadotropic axis activation causing the GnRH release at puberty. Initially, at puberty, GnRH is released in low-frequency pulses during the night, but after the maturation of synaptic connections, it matches the adult pattern. In males, the GnRH pulses occur after 2 hours, while in a female, it changes according to the phases of the menstrual cycle. It is clear that the episodic release of GnRH is a general phenomenon. Also, fluctuation in the amplitude and frequency of GnRH bursts plays a vital role in initiating hormonal charges that ultimately regulate the menstrual cycle. The frequency of GnRH bursts is decreased by testosterone and progesterone and increased by estrogens. The frequency (1 pulse of GnRH/60 to 90 mins) during the late follicular phase of the menstrual cycle is increased, culminating in the LH surge. In the secretory phase of the menstrual cycle, the action of progesterone decreases the frequency (1 pulse of GnRH/200mins). At the end of the menstrual cycle, when progesterone and estrogen secretion decreases, the frequency increases. The sensitivity of gonadotropes increases significantly during the midcycle LH surge; this is due to the exposure to pulses of GnRH at a specific frequency, which describes a critical self-priming effect of GnRH that produces a maximum LH response. Thus, changes in GnRH frequency and amplitude alters the synthesis of gonadotropin synthesis and LH and FSH release.

retrieved_docs.title: Precocious Puberty -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Puberty results from the activation and maturation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis. At birth, there is a brief activation of the HPG axis that results in the increased production of steroidal hormones. This activation may result in breast development in females and pubic hair in males. This phenomenon is known as the “mini-puberty of infancy,” which usually regresses over the first two years of life. Although it is considered to be benign, there is very little information on its etiology as well as clinical significance.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sexual Maturity Rating -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The onset of puberty in both males and females relies on the hypothalamus. The intermittent release of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) from the hypothalamus, also known as the hypothalamic pulse generator, signals the release of luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) from the anterior pituitary. [4] There is a belief that the neurotransmitters GABA and kisspeptin inhibit the release of GnRH. As an individual matures, the hypothalamus becomes less sensitive to this inhibition and eventually shifts towards pulsatile surges. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Dwarfism -- Treatment / Management -- Hormonal Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) analogs are used to halt the progression of precocious puberty. When administered, these analogs continuously suppress the release of gonadotropins from the pituitary and further down the gonadal steroids, which were responsible for premature bone maturation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sexual Maturity Rating -- Pathophysiology -- Precocious Puberty
retrieved_docs.content: Premature activation of the HPG axis underlies the etiology of precocious puberty, with the defining characteristic being pubertal characteristics manifesting before age 8 in girls and 9 in boys. While the exact cause of precocious puberty is unknown, congenital malformations and acquired insults have both merited consideration as potential etiologies. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Puberty -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In general, puberty follows a predictable pattern of onset and sequence. However, due to differences in each individual, including environment and genetics, puberty may proceed in a less-than-predictable way. Issues of concern related to puberty include, but are not limited to delayed puberty, precocious (early) puberty, contrasexual pubertal development, premature adrenarche (androgens causing early pubertal changes), premature thelarche in girls, and premature or delayed menarche.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Puberty -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Menarche

retrieved_docs.title: Precocious Puberty -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The decision to treat depends on the age of the child and the progression of puberty. If the child has rapidly progressing symptoms or if bone age is significantly advanced, consider treatment. The main goals of treatment are to preserve the adult height and to alleviate the associated psychosocial stress. GnRH agonists are the standard of care. [6] Many different formulations (intranasal, intramuscular and subcutaneous) of long and short-acting GnRH agonists exist. The choice of the formulation depends on the patient and clinician preference.  In the United States, leuprolide acetate is the most common. It is a depot injection administered every 3 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Puberty -- Pathophysiology -- Precocious Puberty
retrieved_docs.content: Causes of premature puberty unique to males include gonadotropin-secreting tumors and testotoxicosis. Gonadotropin-secreting tumors typically secrete hCG-like components that can have a similar function in signaling to LH, resulting in an incomplete type of premature puberty in boys. Girls, however, need FSH to increase estrogen production in the ovaries and thus, will not have premature development due to this type of tumor alone. Examples of tumors that might produce hCG are hepatomas, teratomas, and germinomas of the pineal gland. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Precocious Puberty -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Precocious puberty is defined as the appearance of secondary sexual characteristics before the age of 8 years in girls and 9 years in boys. [1] Precocious puberty is classified as either central or peripheral precocious puberty. Central precocious puberty is gonadotropin-dependent and occurs due to early activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Peripheral precocious puberty, also known as peripheral precocious puberty, is gonadotropin-independent and occurs due to excess production of sex hormones either from the gonads, the adrenal glands, ectopic or exogenous sources.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Puberty -- Pathophysiology -- Precocious Puberty
retrieved_docs.content: Precocious puberty, or early development of secondary sexual characteristics, is defined as the appearance of secondary sexual characteristics prior to the age of eight in girls or before the age of nine in boys. Precocious puberty can be broken down by pathological location into central or peripheral precocious puberty. Central precocious puberty (CPP) involves activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis, which leads to early but normal pubertal development. CPP is more common in girls, and while most cases are idiopathic, it can be caused by neoplasm, radiation, head trauma, or genetic conditions. [14] Peripheral precocious puberty (PPP) results from an increase in sex steroids that does not come from activation of the HPG axis. Findings in PPP patients typically include a rapid and atypically sequenced pubertal progression. The most notable causes of PPP include McCune-Albright syndrome and testotoxicosis. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Puberty -- Pathophysiology -- Precocious Puberty
retrieved_docs.content: Testotoxicosis is a cause of peripheral precocious puberty in which boys present with early-onset puberty between 2 and 4 years of age. Patients experience early development of secondary sexual characteristics, accelerated growth, and diminished adult height. The cause of testotoxicosis is linked to a heterogenous constitutively activating mutation of the LHCGR gene encoding the luteinizing hormone receptor. Laboratory analysis will show an increased level of sex steroids in the setting of low LH levels. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist is classified as a hormone and related agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2955
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists constitute a class of compounds that stimulate the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone receptor to modulate pituitary hormone release, and triptorelin is a specific agent that belongs to this LHRH‑agonist class. Recognizing triptorelin as a member of the LHRH‑agonist group is essential for understanding its mechanism of action and therapeutic applications in hormone‑dependent conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 675
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic estrogens, plain are classified as estrogens, which are sex hormones and modulators of the genital system. As a synthetic form of these hormones, they function within the same physiological pathways as natural estrogens, influencing reproductive tissue development and function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 768
retrieved_docs.content: SEX HORMONES AND MODULATORS OF THE GENITAL SYSTEM is a broad category that includes both progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system as well as gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants. The progestogen subgroup comprises hormones that act as progesterone-like agents, regulating menstrual cycles, supporting pregnancy, and influencing genital tissue function. Gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants represent another subgroup that directly stimulate ovarian follicle development and ovulation, thereby modulating reproductive physiology. Together, these subcategories illustrate how various sex hormones and related modulators work in concert to control genital system function and fertility, underscoring their importance in reproductive health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1368
retrieved_docs.content: Sex hormones and modulators of the genital system constitute a therapeutic class that encompasses both gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants and combination formulations of progestogens and estrogens. Within this class, progestogens and estrogens in combination represent a distinct subgroup, and fixed combinations of progestogens and estrogens are a specific type of that subgroup.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 567
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants are classified as a subset of sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, indicating that these agents belong to the broader category of hormonal substances that regulate and modulate genital function and reproductive processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1858
retrieved_docs.content: Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain, and synthetic estrogens, plain are both classified as estrogens, which are sex hormones and modulators of the genital system. These entities represent different sources of estrogenic compounds—natural and semisynthetic versus fully synthetic—yet they share the same functional role in regulating hormonal activity and influencing genital system physiology. Recognizing both categories under the broader class of estrogens helps clinicians understand their similar therapeutic effects and potential side‑effect profiles despite differences in origin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1276
retrieved_docs.content: Sex hormones and modulators of the genital system represent a broad therapeutic class that includes both hormonal contraceptives for systemic use and gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants as specific subcategories. Within this framework, hormonal contraceptives for systemic use are identified as a form of emergency contraception (pohotovostní antikoncepce). This hierarchical organization highlights how agents that alter reproductive hormone activity—ranging from systemic contraceptive formulations to ovulation‑stimulating gonadotropins—are grouped under the overarching category of sex hormones and genital system modulators, facilitating a clearer understanding of their roles in reproductive health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2560
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists are classified within the broader category of hormones and related agents, reflecting their role in modulating the endocrine system. Within this class, triptorelin represents a specific member, exemplifying the pharmacologic application of LHRH agonists in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1601
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol is classified as a hormone, reflecting its role as a key endogenous steroid that regulates reproductive physiology. Gonadotropiny, representing the family of gonadotropins, is a subclass within the broader hormone category, indicating that gonadotropins share fundamental hormonal properties. The group termed “Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain” includes estradiol as one of its members, situating estradiol among both naturally occurring and synthetically derived estrogenic compounds used in clinical practice. Choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are both identified as having the structural class gonadotropiny, meaning they belong to the gonadotropin family and share similar molecular architecture and biological functions related to stimulation of the gonads. Together, these relationships delineate how estradiol fits within the hormone hierarchy, how gonadotropins are organized as a hormonal subclass, and how specific agents such as choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are structurally related to the gonadotropin class, informing both pharmacologic classification and therapeutic application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, and they are considered members of the progesterone family. As such, they function as hormonal agents that influence reproductive physiology and genital system regulation, sharing the core structural and functional characteristics of progesterone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: Adenosin, post‑coital contraceptive agents, hypothalamic hormones, insulin Lispro, and linkosamidy are all recognized as relevant qualifiers in the context of metabolism, indicating that these substances are closely linked to metabolic pathways and can influence or be influenced by metabolic processes. Conversely, metabolism itself is pertinent to the physiological stage of puberty (البلوغ), as well as to the pharmacokinetics and activity of penicillins and spiro‑containing compounds (spirosloučeniny). These interconnections underscore the central role of metabolic mechanisms in hormonal regulation, drug metabolism, and developmental biology, highlighting the importance of considering metabolic effects when evaluating therapeutic agents, hormonal function, and growth‑related changes in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 149
retrieved_docs.content: sex‑hormone framework. Collectively, these relationships delineate a comprehensive map of androgen‑estrogen combinations, multiple progestogen subclasses, their contraceptive and endocrine‑therapy formulations, receptor‑modulating agents, and associated biomarker concepts, providing a structured basis for clinical classification, therapeutic decision‑making, and regulatory categorization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 745
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and estrogens represent a combined formulation of male (androgen) and female (estrogen) sex hormones; this combination is classified as a member of the broader “estrogens / testosterone” group and is defined as an instance of “androgens and female sex hormones in combination.” A more complex preparation that adds other drugs to this mixture is also considered a subtype of the same “androgens and female sex hormones in combination” category. The overarching class “SEX HORMONES AND MODULATORS OF THE GENITAL SYSTEM” includes three major subgroups: hormonal contraceptives for systemic use, the basic combination of androgens and female sex hormones, and gonadotropins together with other ovulation‑stimulating agents. Within this hierarchy, hormonal contraceptives for systemic use are identified as a specific type of emergency contraception (pohotovotní antikoncepce). Together, these relationships delineate how various hormone‑based therapies and contraceptive agents are organized within the broader context of sex‑hormone pharmacology and genital system modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2803
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol is classified as a hormone, representing a specific instance within the broader category of hormones.
